name: Stack
tagline: LIFO — last in, first out. Great for matching and spans.
concept:
## The idea

A **stack** is a last-in-first-out (LIFO) data structure. You already know the theory — `push` adds to the top, `pop` removes from the top — but the real power is recognizing **when** a stack is the right tool.

Stacks shine when you need to:

1. **Match or validate nesting** — parentheses, tags, function calls. Each opening symbol waits for its closer, and closers must match in reverse order (LIFO).
2. **Track pending work** — items on the stack represent unfinished business. When you encounter something that "closes" or "resolves" the top item, pop and process.
3. **Find the next greater/smaller element or spans** — monotonic stacks let you answer "what's the next bigger thing to my right?" in one linear pass instead of nested loops.

The pattern: as you scan left-to-right, the stack remembers what's still "open" or "unresolved." Each new element either closes/resolves something on top, or goes on the stack to be resolved later.

## Python you'll pick up here

- Python lists *are* stacks: `stack.append(x)` to push, `stack.pop()` to pop, `stack[-1]` to peek at the top
- No separate stack class needed — `[]` is both list and stack
- `enumerate(arr)` when you need `(index, value)` pairs
- Using a stack to store tuples/pairs: `stack.append((value, min_so_far))`
- Building result arrays in reverse or with placeholders when the stack gives you answers out of order
whenToUse: You need to match/validate nested or paired structures (parentheses, tags, brackets)
You're tracking 'pending' or 'unresolved' items that close/resolve in reverse order
You want the next greater/smaller element, or spans where something is dominant
The problem mentions 'valid', 'balanced', 'most recent unmatched', or feels recursive but needs iteration

problems.title: Valid Parentheses
problems.keyIdeas: Push each opening bracket onto the stack. When you see a closing bracket, pop the stack and check it's the matching opener. If the stack is empty when you try to pop, or non-empty at the end, or a mismatch happens, it's invalid. This is the canonical stack problem — matching nested pairs in reverse order.
hints: Every closing bracket must match the most recent unmatched opening bracket — that's exactly what a stack tracks.
Use a dictionary to map each closing bracket to its opener: `{')': '(', ']': '[', '}': '{'}`. When you see a closer, pop and compare.
Edge cases: trying to pop from an empty stack (return False immediately), or stack not empty at the end (unmatched openers).

solutions.label: Optimal
solutions.keyIdea: Stack holds unmatched openers; each closer must match the top. One pass, O(n) time.

problems.description: Given a string s containing just the characters '(', ')', '{', '}', '[' and ']', determine if the input string is valid.

problems.title: Min Stack
problems.keyIdeas: A regular stack plus a parallel 'min stack' that tracks the minimum at each level. When you push, also push the new min (either the new value or the current min, whichever is smaller). When you pop, pop both stacks. `getMin()` just peeks the top of the min stack. All operations stay O(1).
hints: You can't just store one global min — when you pop the min, you need to know what the min was *before* that element was pushed.
Keep a second stack that, at each level, stores the minimum value in the main stack up to that point.
When pushing val, push val onto the main stack, and push min(val, current_min) onto the min stack.

solutions.label: Optimal — two stacks
solutions.keyIdea: Maintain a parallel min stack; each level remembers the min at that point in time.

problems.description: Design a stack that supports push, pop, top, and retrieving the minimum element in constant time.

problems.title: Evaluate Reverse Polish Notation
problems.keyIdeas: RPN (postfix notation) means operands come before the operator: `3 4 +` means `3 + 4`. Push numbers onto the stack; when you hit an operator, pop two operands, apply the op, push the result back. At the end, the stack has one element — the answer. The stack naturally handles nested operations because inner results get pushed back and become operands for outer ops.
hints: RPN is designed for stack evaluation: numbers wait on the stack until their operator arrives.
When you see an operator, pop twice: the first pop is the right operand, the second is the left (order matters for - and /).
Python division: `int(a / b)` truncates toward zero, which matches LeetCode's expectation for negative results.

solutions.label: Optimal
solutions.keyIdea: Stack holds operands; each operator consumes two and pushes the result.

problems.description: You are given an array of strings tokens that represents an arithmetic expression in a Reverse Polish Notation.

problems.title: Generate Parentheses
problems.keyIdeas: This is backtracking, but it's in the stack chapter because the valid-generation rule mirrors stack logic: you can add `(` if `open < n`, and `)` if `close < open`. The second condition ensures you never close something that isn't open — exactly the stack invariant from Valid Parentheses. Build each combination recursively, tracking open and close counts.
hints: Think recursively: at each step, you can add '(' or ')' — but only if it keeps the string valid so far.
You can add '(' as long as you haven't used all n of them. You can add ')' only if it wouldn't close more than you've opened.
Base case: when the string has length 2n, it's a complete valid combination — add it to the result.

solutions.label: Optimal — backtracking
solutions.keyIdea: Recursively add '(' or ')' only when it maintains the stack-valid invariant: close < open.
solutions.code:
class Solution:
    def generateParenthesis(self, n: int) -> list[str]:
        res = []
        
        def backtrack(s, open_count, close_count):
            if len(s) == 2 * n:  # complete combination
                res.append(s)
                return
            
            if open_count < n:  # can still add '('
                backtrack(s + '(', open_count + 1, close_count)
            if close_count < open_count:  # can add ')' without breaking validity
                backtrack(s + ')', open_count, close_count + 1)
        
        backtrack('', 0, 0)
        return res
solutions.timeComplexity: O(4^n / sqrt(n)) — the nth Catalan number

solutions.label: Alternative — BFS
solutions.keyIdea: Build strings level-by-level using a queue, maintaining (string, open_count, close_count) state. Reach for this when you want iterative construction or need to process generation in breadth-first order (e.g., to find all strings of a certain length first). Backtracking is more natural for this problem, but BFS avoids recursion stack overhead.
solutions.code:
class Solution:
    def generateParenthesis(self, n: int) -> list[str]:
        from collections import deque
        res = []
        queue = deque([('', 0, 0)])  # (current_string, open_count, close_count)
        
        while queue:
            s, open_count, close_count = queue.popleft()
            
            if len(s) == 2 * n:  # complete valid combination
                res.append(s)
                continue
            
            if open_count < n:  # can add '('
                queue.append((s + '(', open_count + 1, close_count))
            if close_count < open_count:  # can add ')'
                queue.append((s + ')', open_count, close_count + 1))
        
        return res
solutions.timeComplexity: O(4^n / sqrt(n)) — the nth Catalan number

problems.description: Given n pairs of parentheses, write a function to generate all combinations of well-formed parentheses.

problems.title: Daily Temperatures
problems.keyIdeas: This introduces the **monotonic stack** — a stack that stays sorted. Here, store indices of days with descending temperatures. When you see a warmer day, it resolves all the waiting (cooler) days on the stack: pop each and record the distance. If it's not warmer, push the current index to wait for *its* warmer day. One pass, O(n), because each index is pushed and popped at most once.
hints: Brute force: for each day, scan forward until you find a warmer day. That's O(n²).
A stack can remember 'unresolved' days (ones that haven't found their warmer day yet). What should you store: the temperature or the index?
Store indices. As you scan, if today's temp is warmer than the temp at stack[-1], pop that index, compute the wait (current index - popped index), and repeat until the stack top is >= today or empty.

solutions.label: Brute force
solutions.keyIdea: For each day, scan forward until a warmer day is found.

solutions.label: Optimal — monotonic stack
solutions.keyIdea: Stack holds indices of unresolved (cooler) days in descending temp order. A warmer day resolves them all.

problems.description: Given an array of integers temperatures represents the daily temperatures, return an array answer such that answer[i] is the number of days you have to wait after the ith day to get a warmer temperature. If there is no future day for which this is possible, keep answer[i] == 0 instead.

problems.title: Car Fleet
problems.keyIdeas: A fleet forms when a faster car catches a slower one and must slow down. Sort cars by starting position (rightmost first), compute time to target for each. Scan from right to left: if a car's time <= the previous car's time (the stack top), it catches up and joins that fleet (don't push). Otherwise it's a new fleet (push). The stack size at the end is the number of fleets. The stack is effectively tracking 'lead cars' of fleets.
hints: Cars that start ahead can never be caught by cars behind if they're going the same speed or faster, but a car behind can catch up if it's faster.
Compute time-to-target for each car: (target - position) / speed. Sort by position descending (closest to target first).
Scan sorted cars: if current car's time is <= top of stack (the fleet ahead), it joins that fleet (don't push). Otherwise, it's a new fleet (push). Count the stacks at the end.

solutions.label: Optimal — sort + monotonic stack
solutions.keyIdea: Sort by position; stack tracks fleet lead times. A car joins the fleet ahead if its time <= lead's time.

problems.description: There are n cars at given miles away from the starting mile 0, traveling to reach the mile target.

problems.title: 132 Pattern
problems.keyIdeas: Scan right-to-left, maintaining a monotonic decreasing stack to find the pattern. Track the 'second largest' value (the '3' in 1-3-2) as you pop smaller elements. If you encounter any number smaller than this second largest, you've found a valid 1-3-2 pattern. The stack efficiently maintains candidates for the '2' position while tracking the largest '3' that has a valid '2' after it.
hints: Consider the constraints
Think about the optimal approach

solutions.label: Solution from repo
solutions.keyIdea: Scan right-to-left maintaining a decreasing stack. Track the largest '2' value found so far; if we see a smaller number, it's a valid '1'.

problems.description: Given an array of n integers nums, a 132 pattern is a subsequence of three integers nums[i], nums[j] and nums[k] such that i < j < k and nums[i] < nums[k] < nums[j].

problems.title: Trap Rain Water
problems.keyIdeas: Doesnt work properly in case of duplicate heights as heapsort is not stable O(nlogn) since heap pop takes logn n             #iterate left                 #iterate only is the buildings are smaller than the current tall building             #iterate right
hints: Consider the constraints
Think about the optimal approach

problems.title: daily Temperatures O R Next Higher Temperature
problems.keyIdeas: Monotonic stack scanned right-to-left keeps indices of temperatures in increasing order. For each day, pop all temperatures <= current temperature (they're not the answer), then the stack top is the next warmer day. Each index is pushed and popped at most once, giving O(n) time. This is the same problem as the main 'Daily Temperatures' but solved backwards.
hints: Consider the constraints
Think about the optimal approach

solutions.label: Solution from repo
solutions.keyIdea: Scan right-to-left with a monotonic increasing stack. Pop temperatures <= current, the remaining top is the next warmer day.
solutions.code:
def dailyTemperatures(temperature):
        
    stack = [(0,0)]
    n = len(temperatures)
    nextHigherTempArr = [0 for _ in range(n)]

    for i in range(n-1, -1, -1):
        
        while len(stack)>1 and temperatures[i] >= stack[len(stack)-1][0]:
            stack.pop()
        
        nextHigherTempArr[i] = max(0, stack[len(stack)-1][1] - i)
        
        stack.append((temperatures[i], i))
        
    return nextHigherTempArr
    
    
    
    
    
    
    
    
    
    
    
temperatures = [230,160,90]
print(dailyTemperatures(temperatures))

problems.title: maximal Rectangle
problems.keyIdeas: Key Idea 1: Use Largest Rectangle in Histogram Idea Key Idea 2: Use prefixSum approach to compute all histogram arrays
hints: Consider the constraints
Think about the optimal approach

solutions.label: Solution from repo
solutions.keyIdea: Key Idea 1: Use Largest Rectangle in Histogram Idea Key Idea 2: Use prefixSum approach to compute all histogram arrays

problems.description: Given a rows x cols binary matrix filled with 0's and 1's, find the largest rectangle containing only 1's and return its area.

problems.title: minimum Number Of Incrementson Subarraysto Form A Target Array
problems.keyIdeas: Key insight: each increment operation increases a contiguous subarray by 1. The minimum operations needed equals the sum of all 'uphill climbs' in the array. Use a monotonic stack to track when values increase — count only the increments (num - stack[-1]) when going uphill. When going downhill, pop the stack (those increments were already counted). This works because you can 'reuse' an increment operation across multiple elements in a subarray.
hints: Consider the constraints
Think about the optimal approach

solutions.label: Solution from repo
solutions.keyIdea: Track uphill climbs with a stack. Count operations only when increasing; pop when decreasing to reuse previous operations.

problems.title: next Greater Element
problems.keyIdeas: Classic monotonic stack problem: scan right-to-left maintaining a decreasing stack of unresolved elements. For each element, pop all smaller/equal values (they can't be the 'next greater' for this element), then the stack top is the answer. Push current element to become a candidate for future elements. O(n) because each element is pushed and popped at most once.
hints: Consider the constraints
Think about the optimal approach

solutions.label: Solution from repo
solutions.keyIdea: Right-to-left scan with monotonic decreasing stack. Pop smaller elements, stack top is the next greater element.

problems.title: remove Duplicate Letters
problems.keyIdeas: Build the lexicographically smallest result greedily using a monotonic increasing stack. For each character, if it's smaller than the stack top AND the stack top appears later in the string, pop the stack top (defer it for later). This ensures we pick the smallest character at each position while maintaining the constraint that all characters must appear. Track last occurrence of each char to know if popping is safe.
hints: Consider the constraints
Think about the optimal approach

solutions.label: Solution from repo
solutions.keyIdea: Monotonic stack with greedy selection. Pop larger characters only if they appear later; defer them to get lexicographically smaller result.

problems.description: Given a string s, remove duplicate letters so that every letter appears once and only once. You must make sure your result is the smallest in lexicographical order among all possible results.

problems.title: sliding Window Maximum
problems.keyIdeas: Divide array into blocks of size k. For each block, precompute left-to-right max (leftMax) and right-to-left max (rightMax). For any window [i, i+k-1], the maximum is max(rightMax[i], leftMax[i+k-1]) — the max from i to its block end, and from (i+k-1)'s block start to i+k-1. This avoids recomputing max for every window (O(n) vs O(nk)). Alternative approach uses a deque to maintain decreasing order of candidates.
hints: Consider the constraints
Think about the optimal approach

solutions.label: Solution from repo
solutions.keyIdea: Precompute block-wise left and right max arrays. For each window, combine the relevant left and right maxes in O(1).
solutions.code:
nums = [1,3,-1,-3,5,3,6,7]
k = 3


def slidingWindowMaximum(nums, k):
    leftMaxArray = []
    leftMax = -10e10
    n = len(nums)
    for i in range(0, n):
        if i%k==0:
            leftMax = -10e10
        leftMax = max(leftMax, nums[i])
        leftMaxArray.append(leftMax)

    rightMaxArray = []
    rightMax = -10e10
    for i in range(n-1, -1, -1):
        rightMax = max(rightMax, nums[i])
        rightMaxArray.append(rightMax)
        if i%k==0:
            rightMax = -10e10
    return [max(rightMaxArray[i],leftMaxArray[i+k-1]) for i in range(n-k+1)]

print(slidingWindowMaximum(nums, k))

problems.description: You are given an array of integers nums, there is a sliding window of size k which is moving from the very left of the array to the very right. You can only see the k numbers in the window. Each time the sliding window moves right by one position.

problems.title: stock Spanner
problems.keyIdeas: Monotonic decreasing stack tracks unresolved days (prices that haven't found a greater/equal price yet). For each new price, pop all smaller prices from the stack — those days are now resolved. The span is the distance from the current day to the index at the new stack top. This efficiently computes how many consecutive days (including today) had price <= today's price. Amortized O(1) per call since each day is pushed and popped at most once.
hints: Consider the constraints
Think about the optimal approach

solutions.label: Solution from repo
solutions.keyIdea: Monotonic decreasing stack of (price, index) pairs. Pop smaller prices, span is distance to new stack top.

problems.title: longest Valid Parantheses
problems.keyIdeas: Key Idea 1: Max Length ending at index will depend on max length ending before the current match (())() => dp[5] depends on dp[3] Key Idea 2: Use stacks to keep track of unused '(' to match with ')' [in our example index 4] Key Idea 3: Stack top's index will be just right of prev ')' [in our example index 3]
hints: Consider the constraints
Think about the optimal approach

solutions.label: Solution from repo
solutions.keyIdea: Key Idea 1: Max Length ending at index will depend on max length ending before the current match (())() => dp[5] depends on dp[3] Key Idea 2: Use stacks to keep track of unused '(' to match with ')' [in our example index 4] Key Idea 3: Stack top's index will be just right of prev ')' [in our example index 3]

problems.title: Largest Rectangle in Histogram
problems.keyIdeas: Use a monotonic increasing stack (stores indices of bars in increasing height order). When you hit a shorter bar, it closes all taller bars on the stack — for each popped bar, the width of its rectangle extends from the new stack top (exclusive) to the current index (exclusive), and height is the popped bar's height. This efficiently finds the largest rectangle each bar can be part of in one pass. The stack allows you to know the 'boundaries' (left and right limits) for each bar's rectangle without nested loops.
hints: Brute force: for each bar, expand left and right as far as the height allows, compute area. That's O(n²).
A bar's max rectangle is limited by the first shorter bar on each side. A stack can track bars in increasing height order.

Store (index, height) pairs. When current height < stack top, pop and compute area: height = popped bar's height, width = current index - (new stack top index) - 1. Keep popping until the stack is increasing again, then push current bar.

solutions.label: Brute force
solutions.keyIdea: For each bar, expand outward while height permits, compute that rectangle's area.

solutions.label: Optimal — monotonic increasing stack
solutions.keyIdea: Stack holds bars in increasing height order. A shorter bar closes all taller bars, computing their max rectangles.

problems.description: Given an array of integers heights representing the histogram's bar height where the width of each bar is 1, return the area of the largest rectangle in the histogram.
